questions.question_text:
The main dataset used in this exam is the Palmer Penguins dataset described in Table 1. The dataset contains M = 6 attributes for three species of penguins: x1 = Bill length (millimeters), x2 = Bill depth (millimeters), x3 = Flipper length (millimeters), x4 = Body mass (grams), x5 = Penguin sex (1=male, 2=female), x6 = Study year (2007, 2008, or 2009), and the output y = Species (y=1 Adelie, y=2 Gentoo, y=3 Chinstrap). We will consider the type of an attribute as the highest level it obtains in the type-hierarchy (nominal, ordinal, interval and ratio). Which one of the following statements is true about the types of the attributes x1, ..., x6 and the output y in the Palmer Penguins dataset?

A. All attributes except x5 (Sex) are ratio.
B. x5 (Sex) and x6 (Year) are both ordinal.
C. x5 (Sex) and y (Species) are both nominal.
D. x4 (Mass) and x6 (Year) are both interval.

options.A: All attributes except x5 (Sex) are ratio.
options.B: x5 (Sex) and x6 (Year) are both ordinal.
options.C: x5 (Sex) and y (Species) are both nominal.
options.D: x4 (Mass) and x6 (Year) are both interval.

questions.source_location: exam p.2, Question 1 (uses Table 1 attribute descriptions, given in stem)

questions.question_text:
Table 2 contains the summary statistics of the four first attributes (x1, x2, x3, x4) from the Palmer Penguins dataset. Figure 1 shows boxplots corresponding to three of these variables (but not necessarily in that order). Which boxplots in Figure 1 match which attributes?

A. Attribute Bill length corresponds to boxplot 1, Bill depth corresponds to boxplot 2, and Flipper length corresponds to boxplot 3.
B. Attribute Bill length corresponds to boxplot 1, Flipper length corresponds to boxplot 2, and Bill depth corresponds to boxplot 3.
C. Attribute Flipper length corresponds to boxplot 1, Mass corresponds to boxplot 2, and Bill length corresponds to boxplot 3.
D. Attribute Bill depth corresponds to boxplot 1, Flipper length corresponds to boxplot 2, and Bill length corresponds to boxplot 3.

options.A: Attribute Bill length corresponds to boxplot 1, Bill depth corresponds to boxplot 2, and Flipper length corresponds to boxplot 3.
options.B: Attribute Bill length corresponds to boxplot 1, Flipper length corresponds to boxplot 2, and Bill depth corresponds to boxplot 3.
options.C: Attribute Flipper length corresponds to boxplot 1, Mass corresponds to boxplot 2, and Bill length corresponds to boxplot 3.
options.D: Attribute Bill depth corresponds to boxplot 1, Flipper length corresponds to boxplot 2, and Bill length corresponds to boxplot 3.

questions.source_location: exam p.2, Figure 1 (three boxplots) and Table 2 (summary statistics)

questions.question_text:
Figure 2 shows boxplots of the six attributes (x1, x2, x3, x4, x5, x6) from the Palmer Penguins dataset. The attributes are standardized (i.e., the mean has been subtracted and the attributes divided by their standard deviations). Which one of the following statements about the original dataset can be concluded from the boxplots (i.e., based only on information regarding the data provided by Figure 2)?

A. The variance of Bill length is larger than the variance of Flipper length.
B. For Flipper length the mean and median values coincide.
C. There are more male penguins than female penguins.
D. Bill length and Bill depth have positive correlation.

options.A: The variance of Bill length is larger than the variance of Flipper length.
options.B: For Flipper length the mean and median values coincide.
options.C: There are more male penguins than female penguins.
options.D: Bill length and Bill depth have positive correlation.

questions.source_location: exam p.3, Figure 2 (boxplots of six standardized attributes)

questions.question_text:
Figure 3 shows a scatterplot for the two attributes x3 (Flipper length) and x4 (Mass) of the Palmer Penguins dataset. The two attributes have a positive correlation coefficient rho = 0.87 and covariance cov(x3, x4) = 9852. Which of the following are the best estimates for the variance of x3 and variance of x4?

A. sigma^2_x3 = 196 and sigma^2_x4 = 648025
B. sigma^2_x3 = 38 and sigma^2_x4 = 298
C. sigma^2_x3 = 648025 and sigma^2_x4 = 196
D. sigma^2_x3 = 298 and sigma^2_x4 = 38
questions.source_location: exam p.3, Figure 3 (scatterplot of x3 vs x4)

questions.question_text:
A Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is carried out on the Palmer Penguins dataset based on the attributes x1, x2, x3, x4. The data is standardized by (i) subtracting the mean and (ii) dividing each column by its standard deviation to obtain the standardized data matrix Xtilde. A singular value decomposition is then carried out on the standardized data matrix to obtain the decomposition U S V^T = Xtilde, where

V =
[ 0.45  -0.60  -0.64   0.15 ]
[ -0.40 -0.80   0.43  -0.16 ]
[ 0.58  -0.01   0.24  -0.78 ]
[ 0.55  -0.08   0.59   0.58 ]

S =
[ 30.19   0.0    0.0    0.0  ]
[ 0.0    16.08   0.0    0.0  ]
[ 0.0     0.0   11.07   0.0  ]
[ 0.0     0.0    0.0    5.98 ]

Which one of the following statements is correct?

A. The first principal component accounts for less than 50 percent of the variance.
B. The first two principal components account for more than 90 percent of the variance.
C. The first three principal components account for more than 95 percent of the variance.
D. The last principal component accounts for more than 3 percent of the variance.

options.A: The first principal component accounts for less than 50 percent of the variance.
options.B: The first two principal components account for more than 90 percent of the variance.
options.C: The first three principal components account for more than 95 percent of the variance.
options.D: The last principal component accounts for more than 3 percent of the variance.

questions.source_location: exam p.4, Question 5 (V and S matrices given inline as text)

questions.question_text:
Consider a principal component analysis (PCA) carried out on the standardized Palmer Penguins dataset, with

V =
[ 0.45  -0.60  -0.64   0.15 ]
[ -0.40 -0.80   0.43  -0.16 ]
[ 0.58  -0.01   0.24  -0.78 ]
[ 0.55  -0.08   0.59   0.58 ]

Recall that the d'th column of V defines the d'th principal component direction. The four features are x1 (Bill length), x2 (Bill depth), x3 (Flipper length), x4 (Body mass). Which one of the following statements is correct?

A. Any single observation with a high negative projection onto the second principal component will in general have high negative values for all four features x1, x2, x3, x4.
B. The first column vector of V is longer than the second column vector of V measured by the Euclidean norm (2-norm).
C. The first principal component primarily separates penguins with relatively short Flipper length and high Body mass from penguins with relatively long Flipper length and low Body mass.
D. The fourth principal component primarily separates penguins with relatively short Flipper length and high Body mass from penguins with relatively long Flipper length and low Body mass.

options.A: Any single observation with a high negative projection onto the second principal component will in general have high negative values for all four features x1, x2, x3, x4.
options.B: The first column vector of V is longer than the second column vector of V measured by the Euclidean norm (2-norm).
options.C: The first principal component primarily separates penguins with relatively short Flipper length and high Body mass from penguins with relatively long Flipper length and low Body mass.
options.D: The fourth principal component primarily separates penguins with relatively short Flipper length and high Body mass from penguins with relatively long Flipper length and low Body mass.

questions.source_location: exam p.4, Question 6 (uses V matrix from Question 5, given inline as text)

questions.question_text:
A principal component analysis (PCA) is carried out on the standardized Palmer Penguins dataset (attributes x1 Bill length, x2 Bill depth, x3 Flipper length, x4 Body mass), where the singular value decomposition of the standardized data matrix gives

V =
[ 0.45  -0.60  -0.64   0.15 ]
[ -0.40 -0.80   0.43  -0.16 ]
[ 0.58  -0.01   0.24  -0.78 ]
[ 0.55  -0.08   0.59   0.58 ]

and the d'th column of V defines the d'th principal component direction. Figure 4 shows 2D scatter plots for the Palmer Penguin dataset projected onto different combinations of the principal components. The class labels y (penguin species Adelie, Gentoo, Chinstrap) are indicated in the plots. Consider a new observation x for which the four features x1, x2, x3, x4 are standardized, resulting in the point xtilde with coordinates xtilde1 = -1, xtilde2 = -1, xtilde3 = -1, xtilde4 = 1. Consider the point projected onto the principal components. Furthermore, consider a k-nearest neighbor classification with k = 1 using the Euclidean distance measure and using the projected dataset shown in the individual 2D plots as the training data. In which one of the following 2D projections (shown in Figure 4) will the 1-nearest neighbor classifier classify the point xtilde as a Chinstrap?

A. Data projected onto PC1 and PC4.
B. Data projected onto PC2 and PC4.
C. Data projected onto PC2 and PC3.
D. Data projected onto PC3 and PC4.

options.A: Data projected onto PC1 and PC4.
options.B: Data projected onto PC2 and PC4.
options.C: Data projected onto PC2 and PC3.
options.D: Data projected onto PC3 and PC4.

questions.source_location: exam p.5, Figure 4 (six 2D PCA projection scatter plots)

questions.question_text:
The k-means algorithm with k = 3 was applied to the Palmer Penguins dataset. Figure 5 shows the confusion matrix, where the cluster assignment obtained by the k-means algorithm is compared to the ground truth class label. We wish to compare the quality of the k-means clustering, Z, to the ground truth clustering, Q. What is the Rand index (Rand similarity) between Z and Q?

A. R(Z,Q) = 0.72
B. R(Z,Q) = 0.79
C. R(Z,Q) = 0.87
D. R(Z,Q) = 0.96
questions.text_representation:
Confusion matrix (Figure 5). Rows are the actual class, columns are the k-means cluster number (k = 3). Entries are observation counts:

              Cluster 1   Cluster 2   Cluster 3
Adelie         114          0          32
Gentoo           0        119           0
Chinstrap        8          0          60
questions.source_location: exam p.6, Figure 5 (3x3 confusion matrix, counts printed)

questions.question_text:
Which one of the following machine-learning models is not trained by fitting any parameters?

A. Linear regression applied to data with one attribute.
B. Neural network with no hidden layers.
C. K nearest neighbor classification using the K = 1 nearest neighbour classification rule.
D. Multinomial regression applied to data with three output classes.

options.A: Linear regression applied to data with one attribute.
options.B: Neural network with no hidden layers.
options.C: K nearest neighbor classification using the K = 1 nearest neighbour classification rule.
options.D: Multinomial regression applied to data with three output classes.

questions.source_location: exam p.6, Question 9

questions.question_text:
Three of the following actions will typically reduce the amount of over-fitting, and one of them will typically increase it. Which option will typically increase the amount of over-fitting?

A. Reduce the number of attributes.
B. Reduce the amount of training data.
C. Select a less complex model.
D. Add model regularisation.

options.B: Reduce the amount of training data.

questions.source_location: exam p.7, Question 10

questions.question_text:
Consider the Palmer Penguins dataset. We wish to predict the species based on the attributes Bill length and Bill depth using a Bayes classifier. Suppose the attributes have been binarized such that xhat1 = 0 corresponds to x1 <= 44.5 (and otherwise xhat1 = 1) and xhat2 = 0 corresponds to x2 <= 17.3 (and otherwise xhat2 = 1). Suppose the probability for each of the configurations of xhat1 and xhat2 conditional on the species y are as given in Table 3 and the prior probability of the species are p(y=1) = 0.44, p(y=2) = 0.36, p(y=3) = 0.20. Using this, what is then the probability an observation is Chinstrap (y=3) given that xhat1 = 1 and xhat2 = 0?

A. p(y=3 | xhat1=1, xhat2=0) = 0.033
B. p(y=3 | xhat1=1, xhat2=0) = 0.085
C. p(y=3 | xhat1=1, xhat2=0) = 0.095
D. p(y=3 | xhat1=1, xhat2=0) = 0.158
questions.text_representation:
Table 3: probability of observing particular values of xhat1 and xhat2 conditional on y, i.e. p(xhat1, xhat2 | y).

                       y=1     y=2     y=3
xhat1=0, xhat2=0       0.23    0.15    0.07
xhat1=0, xhat2=1       0.75    0.00    0.01
xhat1=1, xhat2=0       0.00    0.85    0.16
xhat1=1, xhat2=1       0.02    0.00    0.76

Priors: p(y=1) = 0.44, p(y=2) = 0.36, p(y=3) = 0.20.
questions.source_location: exam p.7, Table 3 (conditional probability table)

questions.question_text:
Table 4 shows the pairwise Euclidean distances between 10 observations o1, o2, ..., o10 from the Palmer Penguins dataset. To examine if observation o2 may be an outlier, we will calculate the average relative density using the Euclidean distance based on the observations given in Table 4 only. We recall that the KNN density and average relative density (ard) for the observation xi are given by:

density(xi, K) = 1 / ( (1/K) * sum over x' in N(xi,K) of d(xi, x') )
ard(xi, K) = density(xi, K) / ( (1/K) * sum over xj in N(xi,K) of density(xj, K) )

where N(xi, K) is the set of K nearest neighbors of observation xi excluding the i'th observation. What is the average relative density for observation o2 for K = 2 nearest neighbors?

A. 0.01
B. 0.37
C. 0.68
D. 0.73
questions.source_location: exam p.8, Table 4 (10x10 pairwise distance matrix)

questions.question_text:
Again, consider the pairwise Euclidean distances between the 10 observations o1, o2, ..., o10 from the Palmer Penguins dataset in Table 4. Consider the two clusters C2 = {o6, o7} and C3 = {o8, o9, o10}. What is the distance between C2 and C3 using average linkage as the linkage function?

A. d(C2, C3) approx 1108.3
B. d(C2, C3) approx 1145.8
C. d(C2, C3) approx 1183.3
D. d(C2, C3) approx 1450.0
questions.source_location: exam p.8, Table 4 (10x10 pairwise distance matrix)
exam p.9, Figure 6 (dendrogram, maximum linkage)

questions.question_text:
Table 5 shows N = 10 observations from the Palmer Penguins dataset. The data is processed to produce four new binary features such that fi = 1 corresponds to a value xi greater than the median. We make the following conditional independence assumption about the binary features: p(f1, f2, f3, f4 | x5) = p(f1 | x5) p(f2, f3 | x5) p(f4 | x5). Using this assumption and Bayes' rule, we obtain the classifier

p(x5 | f1, f2, f3, f4) = [ p(f1|x5) p(f2,f3|x5) p(f4|x5) p(x5) ] / [ sum over k=1..2 of p(f1|x5=k) p(f2,f3|x5=k) p(f4|x5=k) p(x5=k) ].

Consider a new observation o21 with binary feature values f1 = 0, f2 = 0, f3 = 1, f4 = 0. What is the probability that o21 is classified as male (x5 = 1) using the classifier above?

A. p(x5=1 | f1=0, f2=0, f3=1, f4=0) = 1/10
B. p(x5=1 | f1=0, f2=0, f3=1, f4=0) = 1/5
C. p(x5=1 | f1=0, f2=0, f3=1, f4=0) = 1/2
D. p(x5=1 | f1=0, f2=0, f3=1, f4=0) = 10/19
questions.text_representation:
Table 5: binarized version of 10 observations from the Palmer Penguins dataset. fi = 1 if feature xi is greater than the median (otherwise 0). x5 is the sex of the penguin (1 = male, 2 = female).

        f1   f2   f3   f4   x5
o11      0    0    1    0    1
o12      1    0    1    0    1

o20      1    0    0    0    2

New observation o21: f1 = 0, f2 = 0, f3 = 1, f4 = 0.

questions.question_text:
We again consider the binary matrix from Table 5 as a market basket problem consisting of N = 10 transactions o11, ..., o20 and M = 4 items f1, ..., f4. What is the confidence of the rule {f1, f4} -> {f3}?

A. The confidence is 1/10
B. The confidence is 3/10
C. The confidence is 7/10
D. The confidence is 1

        f1   f2   f3   f4

questions.question_text:
Consider classifying the Palmer Penguins dataset according to the species attribute y. The dataset contains 146 observations for y = 1 (Adelie), 119 observations for y = 2 (Gentoo) and 68 observations for y = 3 (Chinstrap). During training of a decision tree, the classification error has been used as impurity measure classError(v) = 1 - max_c p(c|v), where p(c|v) denotes the fraction of observations belonging to class c at a given node v. The tree is constructed by Hunts algorithm using the purity gain Delta = classError(parent) - sum over k=1..2 of (N(vk)/N) classError(vk), where N is the total number of observations at the parent node and N(vk) is the number of observations associated with the k-th child node vk. After the first split you learn that the left node contains all the Adelie and Gentoo observations while the right node contains all the Chinstrap observations. What is the purity gain for the split?

A. The purity gain is 1/5.
B. The purity gain is 68/333.
C. The purity gain is 68/265.
D. The purity gain is 265/333.
questions.source_location: exam p.11, Question 18 (counts 146/119/68 given inline in stem)

questions.question_text:
Consider again the Palmer Penguins dataset. Suppose we limit ourselves to N = 7 observations and to class y = 1 or y = 2, and only consider the features x1 and x2. We use a KNN classification model (K = 3) and apply AdaBoost to improve the performance. After the first round of boosting, we obtain predictions and the true class labels as tabulated in Table 6. Given this information, how will the AdaBoost update the weights w?

A. [0.083  0.083  0.5    0.083  0.083  0.083  0.083]
B. [0.165  0.165  0.008  0.165  0.165  0.165  0.165]
C. [0.152  0.152  0.085  0.152  0.152  0.152  0.152]
D. [0.01   0.01   0.937  0.01   0.01   0.01   0.01]
questions.text_representation:
Table 6: for each of the N = 7 observations (first column), the true class label ytrue (second column) and the predicted output of the AdaBoost classifier for the first round of boosting t = 1 (third column).

Variable   ytrue   t=1
y1          2       2
y2          1       1
y3          1       2
y4          1       1
y5          2       2
y6          2       2
y7          2       2
questions.source_location: exam p.11, Table 6 (7 observations: true vs predicted labels)

questions.question_text:
We consider an artificial dataset of N = 4000 observations. The dataset is classified according to a decision tree of the form shown in Figure 7 resulting in a partition into classes indicated by the colors/markers in Figure 8. What is the correct rule assignment to the nodes in the decision tree?

A. A: ||[x1;x2] - [2;4]||_1 < 3,  B: ||[x1;x2] - [6;4]||_1 < 3,  C: ||[x1;x2] - [4;2]||_1 < 3
B. A: ||[x1;x2] - [6;4]||_1 < 3,  B: ||[x1;x2] - [4;2]||_1 < 3,  C: ||[x1;x2] - [2;4]||_1 < 3
C. A: ||[x1;x2] - [6;4]||_1 < 3,  B: ||[x1;x2] - [2;4]||_1 < 3,  C: ||[x1;x2] - [4;2]||_1 < 3
D. A: ||[x1;x2] - [2;4]||_1 < 3,  B: ||[x1;x2] - [4;2]||_1 < 3,  C: ||[x1;x2] - [6;4]||_1 < 3
questions.source_location: exam p.12, Figure 7 (decision tree) and Figure 8 (classification boundaries scatter plot)

questions.question_text:
Consider again the Palmer Penguins dataset. To simplify the setup further, we select just 6 observations and train a logistic regression classifier using only the feature x2 as input (as usual, we apply a simple feature transformation to the inputs to add a constant feature in the first coordinate to handle the intercept term). As output, we want to predict the Penguin sex (x5). To be consistent with the lecture notes, we label the output as x5 = 0 (corresponding to male) and x5 = 1 (corresponding to female). In Figure 9 is shown the predicted output probability of an observation belonging to the positive class, p(x5 = 1 | x2). What are the weights?

A. w = [423.49; 48.16]
B. w = [0.0; -46.21]
C. w = [0.0; -27.89]
D. w = [418.94; -26.12]
questions.source_location: exam p.13, Figure 9 (logistic regression output curve)

questions.question_text:
We fit a GMM to a single feature x2 from the Palmer Penguins dataset. Recall the density of a 1D GMM is p(x) = sum over k=1..K of wk N(x | muk, sigmak^2), and suppose that the identified values of the mixture weights are w1 = 0.13, w2 = 0.55, w3 = 0.32 and the parameters of the normal densities: mu1 = 18.347, mu2 = 14.997, mu3 = 18.421, sigma1 = 1.2193, sigma2 = 0.986, sigma3 = 1.1354. According to the GMM, what is the probability an observation at x0 = 15.38 is assigned to cluster k = 2?

A. 0.975
B. 0.389
C. 0.213
D. 0.042
questions.source_location: exam p.13, Question 22 (all GMM parameters given inline as text)

questions.question_text:
We will consider the Palmer Penguins dataset and two models for predicting the class label y. Let M1 be a K = 1 nearest neighbor classification model and M2 a K = 5 nearest neighbor classification model. To compare them statistically, we perform K = 3 fold cross-validation, and for each fold we record the number of observations where both models are correct (as M1/M2), M1 is correct and M2 wrong (as M1/M2bar), and so on. The outcome is in Table 7. We want to test if the two classifiers perform differently. The null hypothesis is that the models have the same performance. Given the values of the binomial cumulative distribution function in Table 8 and assuming that the null hypothesis is true, what is the p-value from McNemar's test (rounded to two decimals)?

A. 0.23
B. 0.45
C. 0.84
D. 0.90
questions.source_location: exam p.14, Table 7 (CV outcome counts) and Table 8 (binomial CDF table)

questions.question_text:
A neural network classifier is trained to distinguish between two classes y in {0, 1} in a small dataset consisting of N = 7 observations. Suppose the true class label y and the predicted probability an observation belongs to class 1, yhat, is as given in Table 9. To evaluate the classifier, we will use the area under curve (AUC) of the receiver operator characteristic (ROC) curve. In Figure 10 is given four proposed ROC curves; which one of the curves corresponds to the classifier?

A. ROC curve 1
B. ROC curve 2
C. ROC curve 3
D. ROC curve 4
questions.source_location: exam p.15, Figure 10 (four proposed ROC curves) and Table 9 (true labels + predicted probabilities)

questions.question_text:
We will consider an artificial neural network (ANN) trained on the Palmer Penguins dataset to predict the class label y based on the first four attributes x1, ..., x4. The neural network has a single hidden layer containing nh = 6 units, and will use the softmax activation function (specifically, the over-parameterized softmax function described in section 15.3.2 of the lecture notes) to predict the class label y since it is a multi-class problem (3 classes). For the hidden layer we will use the tanh non-linear activation function. How many parameters has to be trained to fit the neural network?

A. The network contains 36 parameters
B. The network contains 42 parameters
C. The network contains 51 parameters
D. The network contains 72 parameters
questions.source_location: exam p.15, Question 25

questions.question_text:
Suppose that you have a deep neural network that can binary classify whether an image contains a penguin or not. If an image contains a penguin, the network will correctly classify it as a penguin with probability 97%. If an image does not contain a penguin, the network will classify it as a penguin with probability 3%. You apply the classifier to a dataset where 1% of the images contain a penguin. What is the probability that a random image from this dataset contains a penguin given that it is classified as a penguin?

A. approx 0.97
B. approx 0.75
C. approx 0.50
D. approx 0.25
questions.source_location: exam p.16, Question 26

questions.question_text:
We use a multinomial regression model to predict the species (y) from the six attributes x1, ..., x6 for the Palmer Penguins dataset. We apply least squares regularization (i.e., L2 regularization) to the weights of the multinomial regression model and use two-level cross-validation to select the optimal value of the regularization constant lambda in {0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1.0}. In the outer fold, we use 3-fold cross-validation, and in the inner fold, we use leave-one-out cross-validation. With this setup and N = 333 observations o1, ..., o333 in the dataset, how many times is o1 used for training a model?

A. 999
B. 1768
C. 1770
D. 2667
questions.source_location: exam p.16, Question 27
